hook: A 200-year-old Hokkien courtyard house with a scuba pool in the middle
facts: Built in the early 1800s by a Chinese merchant family that still owns it
One of the last surviving siheyuan-style courtyard homes in Bangkok
The central courtyard now holds a deep diving-school pool
A small cafe lets visitors linger inside the timber compound
w: Two centuries of one family's fortune, raised in the Fujian courtyard style now nearly extinct in Bangkok. The descendants still hold the deeds, but the heart of the timber compound got dug out into a deep pool, so it doubles as a scuba school. Sip coffee where merchants once weighed birds-nest. A house that learned to dive.

hook: Pink-domed Portuguese church anchoring a 250-year Catholic riverside enclave
facts: Founded 1770 after King Taksin granted Portuguese supporters land here
Present pink-domed building dates from 1916
Heart of the Kudeejeen Siamo-Portuguese community
Nearby families still bake khanom farang, a Portuguese-rooted sponge cake
w: Taksin rewarded his Portuguese gunners with this riverbank in 1770, and their descendants never left. The pink dome you see went up in 1916, presiding over lanes where families still bake khanom farang, a sponge cake carrying Lusophone recipes older than the city itself. Catholic Bangkok, raisin-topped and quietly enduring.

hook: Taksin-era Hokkien shrine with a rare seated Guanyin and Three Kingdoms murals
facts: Among Thailand's oldest Chinese shrines, tied to King Taksin's reign 1767-1782
Holds a rare seated, rather than standing, Guanyin image from China
Interior murals depict scenes from Romance of the Three Kingdoms
Won a Siamese Architects preservation award in 2008
w: When Ayutthaya fell, Hokkien Chinese followed Taksin downriver and raised this joss house, giving the whole quarter its name. Inside sits a rare seated Guanyin shipped from China, watched over by wall paintings of Three Kingdoms battles. The carpentry won a preservation prize. Older than Bangkok, and it knows it.

hook: The Museum of Death, once home to a mummified accused cannibal
facts: Cluster of forensic, pathology and anatomy museums inside Siriraj Hospital
Long displayed the embalmed body of Si Quey, an accused serial killer executed 1959
Si Quey became a Thai bogeyman used to scare misbehaving children
His remains were finally cremated in 2020 after public protest
legend: Parents long warned children that Si Quey would come and catch them if they misbehaved
w: Locals call it the Museum of Death, a warren of forensic and anatomy halls inside a working hospital. For decades it kept Si Quey, an executed killer embalmed and labelled cannibal, so terrifying parents threatened children with his name. Public conscience won and he was cremated in 2020. Preserved deformities, and a ghost retired.

hook: A frozen-in-time middle-class home off Charoen Krung, donated by its owner
facts: Centred on a teak house built in 1937 for under 2,400 baht
Shows pre- and post-WWII middle-class Bangkok life, rooms left as lived in
Donated to the city by Professor Waraporn Surawadee
Free to enter, set in a quiet garden compound off Soi 43
w: Down a lane off Charoen Krung, a dark teak house built in 1937 for under 2,400 baht, left almost exactly as a family lived in it before and after the war. Professor Waraporn handed her own home to the city rather than sell it. Crockery, radios, a doctor's surgery, all dust-free and waiting. Ordinary Bangkok, embalmed kindly.

n: Chao Pho Suea Tiger God Shrine
hook: Teochew tiger-god joss house where the faithful offer raw pork and eggs
facts: Established 1834 under King Rama III, moved to Tanao Road under Rama V
Founded by Teochew Chinese immigrants
Devotees offer raw pork, eggs and rice liquor to the Tiger God
Listed as a registered ancient monument since 1988
legend: The Tiger God is believed to grant strength, protection and prosperity
w: Teochew migrants raised this joss house in 1834, and Rama V later shifted it to Tanao Road, near the Giant Swing. People come to the Tiger God for backbone and protection, leaving raw pork, eggs and a splash of rice liquor on the altars. Incense thick enough to lean on. Carnivore offerings for a carnivore deity.

hook: The royal court's Hindu temple, home of Bangkok's surviving Brahmin priests
facts: Established 1784 by King Rama I as the Brahmin office of the Thai court
Stands beside the Giant Swing it served during the now-banned swing rite
The Triyamphawai swinging ceremony stopped in 1935 after fatal falls
Still maintained by court Brahmins for royal Hindu rituals
legend: Priests swung skyward to catch a coin purse, honouring Shiva's visit to earth
w: Hindu priests have served the Thai king from this compound since 1784, abode of the gods in the heart of Buddhist Bangkok. Beside it once stood the swing where Brahmins soared skyward to snatch a coin purse for Shiva, until too many fell to their deaths and the rite was banned in 1935. Tamil hymns, still murmured behind the walls.

hook: A law firm's collection of 4,000 fakes, from watches to brake pads
facts: Run by the law firm Tilleke and Gibbins, started 1989 from about 100 items
Now holds over 4,000 trademark and copyright-infringing goods
Categories span fake drugs, liquor, car parts, cosmetics and clothing
Sits on the 26th floor of Supalai Grand Tower, viewable by appointment
w: Lawyers seized so many fakes in court that they built a museum for them, 26 floors up a Rama III office tower. Four thousand counterfeits sit in cases: knock-off whisky, bogus brake pads, fake heart pills, watches you'd swear were real. An attorney walks you through, dismantling the idea that piracy hurts nobody. Crime, neatly catalogued.

hook: Chinatown's incense-choked Dragon Lotus temple of three faiths at once
facts: Founded 1871-72 as Wat Leng Noei Yi, renamed by King Rama V
Largest Chinese Buddhist temple in Bangkok
Blends Mahayana Buddhism, Taoism and Confucianism under one roof
Epicentre of the city's annual Vegetarian Festival
w: A Teochew monk raised the Dragon Lotus temple in the 1870s, and Rama V gave it its grand name. Buddhist, Taoist and Confucian altars share the smoke-blackened halls, four-metre guardian gods glowering by the door. During the Vegetarian Festival the whole street goes white-clad and meatless. The spiritual furnace of Chinatown.

hook: Lanes of dealers squinting through loupes at lucky Buddhist amulets
facts: Century-old market beside Wat Mahathat and Thammasat University
Specialists grade and trade amulets believed to carry protective power
Stalls sell monk figurines, talismans, antique swords and relics
Buyers seek charms said to deflect bullets, accidents or bad luck
legend: Certain amulets are believed to make the wearer impervious to bullets or to levitate
w: Down lanes beside Wat Mahathat, men crouch over glass cases with jeweller's loupes, grading tiny clay Buddhas the way others grade diamonds. The right amulet, they say, turns bullets and snaps bad luck; magazines catalogue charms said to levitate. Antique swords lean in corners. A bourse where faith trades by the gram.

hook: Canal-side shrine to Thailand's most beloved ghost, a devoted dead wife
facts: Wat Mahabut dates to 1762, near the close of the Ayutthaya era
The Mae Nak shrine draws women praying for safe childbirth or draft exemption
Devotees leave dresses, toys and cosmetics for the spirit and her child
Set under old trees beside the Phra Khanong canal
legend: Legend says Mae Nak died in childbirth yet kept house as a ghost, stretching her arm to impossible length to retrieve a fallen lime
w: Legend says Mae Nak died in childbirth but loved her husband too fiercely to leave, keeping house as a ghost until the day he saw her arm stretch to the floor for a fallen lime. Her canal-side shrine overflows with offered dresses, toys and lipstick. Women pray here for safe births and sons spared the draft. Devotion, undead.

hook: Silk king's teak compound, frozen the day he vanished without trace
facts: Built 1959 from six old teak houses moved from Ayutthaya and Bangkrua
Home of American silk entrepreneur and former OSS officer Jim Thompson
Thompson vanished in Malaysia in 1967 and was never found
Holds his collection of Southeast Asian Buddhist art
legend: Theories about his disappearance range from a jungle accident to kidnapping to a return to spycraft
w: An American spy-turned-silk-magnate stitched six old teak houses into this canal-side compound in 1959, hanging it with Buddhist art. Then in 1967 Jim Thompson walked into Malaysian jungle and vanished, sparking decades of kidnap and spy theories. The house waits, lacquered and silent, as if he might come back for tea. Unsolved, gorgeous.

hook: Thailand's oldest wooden cinema, a barn that played silent films to a brass band
facts: Opened 1918 during the silent era, the oldest standing cinema in Thailand
Built as a two-storey wooden, barn-like hall in Nang Loeng
Showed Thai films until closing for want of repair funds in 1993
Reopened recently for free screenings of vintage classics
w: In 1918 the smart set of Nang Loeng came to this timber barn to watch silent films scored live by a brass band. The reels kept turning until 1993, when the floors sagged and the money ran out. Now restored, it screens old Thai weepies for free under a creaking wooden roof. The country's oldest picture house, blinking awake.

hook: A pocket riverside garden on the slum site where a future queen grew up poor
facts: Opened 1997 on the childhood neighbourhood of the Princess Mother, Srinagarindra
Includes a reconstruction of her modest childhood home
Built in a Khlong San area shared by Chinese, Muslim and Lao communities
Free, quiet and rarely crowded near Phra Pok Klao Bridge
w: On the Thonburi backstreets where a poor girl grew up to mother a king, the city carved out this hush of a garden in 1997. A replica of her plain childhood house sits among the shade trees, hemmed by Chinese, Muslim and Lao neighbours who never moved away. Few tourists find it. A rags-to-monarchy story, planted in green.

hook: Century-old shophouse pouring herbal moonshine to live Thai folk music
facts: Set in a restored two-storey, century-old Chinatown shophouse on Soi Nana
Serves cocktails built on ya dong, local herbal rice spirits
Stages nightly live traditional Thai music
Walls expose gilded brick, a nod to Buddhas once hidden in plaster
legend: Exposed gold brick recalls how Siamese once buried golden Buddhas in plaster to hide them from invaders
w: Grandchildren reclaimed a crumbling Chinatown shophouse and turned it into a den of ya dong, the fierce herbal moonshine usually sold from roadside jars. Drummers and players fill the nights with old Thai music while gilded brick glints from stripped walls, an echo of Buddhas once plastered over to fool invaders. Heritage, served stiff.

hook: Temple with a man-made turtle mountain alive with hundreds of turtles
facts: Built from 1828 on a nobleman's donated coffee garden under King Rama III
Khao Mor, its artificial rock mountain, is among Thailand's oldest and largest
A pond around the mountain teems with turtles that visitors feed
Its 60-metre white chedi won a UNESCO conservation award
w: A nobleman gave up his coffee garden in 1828 to found this Thonburi temple, then built a miniature mountain of craggy rock studded with tiny chedis. The pond circling it churns with sacred turtles, fed bread and fruit by visitors all day. Buddhism reads the turtle as patience and long life. A made mountain, and a thousand slow pilgrims.

hook: Restored 19th-century Chinese trading mansion built around a sea-goddess shrine
facts: A 19th-century Chinese mansion and former Thai-China trading port on the river
Centres on a 160-year-plus shrine to Mazu, goddess of seafarers
Restoration in 2017 uncovered ornate original wall murals
Now a free riverside heritage and creative space
legend: Sailors built the Mazu shrine to beg the sea goddess for safe passage
w: Chinese junks once tied up at this riverside port, and the merchants who settled here raised a shrine to Mazu, who guards sailors, before they built their homes. Left to rot for decades, the mansion was scraped back to its original murals in 2017. The goddess still holds the courtyard. A trading house that prayed before it traded.

hook: The world's second snake farm, milking cobras for antivenom since 1923
facts: Part of the Queen Saovabha Memorial Institute, opened on Rama IV Road in 1922
Snake farm opened 1923, the second ever after Brazil's Instituto Butantan
Staff milk venom from live cobras and vipers in public handling shows
Run by the Thai Red Cross to produce antivenom
w: Imported antivenom failed against Thai snakes, so in 1923 the Red Cross built only the world's second snake farm to milk its own cobras. A century on, handlers still pin hooded snakes barehanded in the pit, squeezing venom into glass before crowds. Behind the show, the serum that saves bite victims nationwide. Medicine, with fangs.

hook: A serene old-town park laid over Bangkok's first maximum-security prison
facts: Site of a prison built 1890 under King Rama V, modelled on Singapore's jails
Inmates were transferred out and the prison closed in the 1990s
Surviving red-brick walls and watchtowers still ring the gardens
Reborn as a public park, later housing a crime and justice museum
w: Joggers and grandmothers now circle lawns where Bangkok's first hard prison stood, built in 1890 on a Singapore model and notorious for floggings and executions. The inmates were moved out in the 1990s, but red-brick walls and stub watchtowers still box in the greenery. A crime museum keeps the grim memory. Punishment, gently overgrown.

hook: A 200-year-old canal house staging rare Thai rod-puppet shows
facts: Wooden house over 200 years old, perched over Khlong Bang Luang
Encloses an Ayutthaya-period chedi within its open ground floor
Hosts traditional Thai hun lakhon lek rod-puppet performances
Reached by long-tail boat along the old Thonburi canals
w: Out where old Thonburi canals still carry long-tails, a 200-year-old wooden house leans over the water, an Ayutthaya-era chedi rising through its open floor. Black-clad puppeteers work delicate rod puppets most afternoons, conjuring Hanuman from folklore. Coffee, masks, and the slap of paddles. The city as it moved before roads.

hook: A rusted Fiat abandoned in an alley, now the icon of a car-parts quarter
facts: An abandoned, unclaimed Fiat 500 rusting in a Talat Noi lane
Sits in the Siang Kong district, decades old centre of used car-part dealers
Chinese workers have refurbished engine parts here for around 70 years
Now a magnet for photographers amid the alleyway street art
w: Nobody came back for the little Fiat, so it sat in a Talat Noi lane until rust ate it into sculpture. Fitting, in a quarter where Chinese mechanics have salvaged and resold engine parts for seventy years, the place nicknamed Siang Kong. Street artists painted around it; photographers queue. An abandoned car that outlived its owner.

hook: A spiky metal-spired monastery, the last of its kind on earth
facts: Part of Wat Ratchanatdaram, begun 1846 under King Rama III
Its 37 metal spires represent the 37 virtues toward enlightenment
Modelled on lost iron monasteries of India and Sri Lanka
Only completed in the 20th century, the world's sole surviving example
w: Rama III started this iron-spired tower in 1846, copying lost monasteries of ancient India and Sri Lanka unseen for a thousand years. Its 37 metal spires count the steps to enlightenment; a maze of columns coils beneath. Finished only last century, it stands as the last of its kind anywhere. A castle made of doctrine.
